title: Resources
description: Declare bounded-concurrency resource pools in factory.json and consume them from workstation requirements while a dispatch is in flight.

whatItCovers.title: What It Covers
whatItCovers.body: Resources are bounded-concurrency pools in factory.json. A top-level resources[] entry declares a named pool with a capacity (and optional typed metadata). A workstation requirement holds that capacity while a dispatch runs, so later matching dispatches wait when the pool is exhausted. This page teaches that pool-and-requirement contract for web readers.

keyConcepts.title: Key Concepts
keyConcepts.body: A resource pool is a shared concurrency limit. Declare it once under top-level resources[] with at least {name, capacity}. Consume it from workstations[].resources[] so the workstation holds capacity for the workflow step while the dispatch is in flight. The same pool name means one shared limit across every requirement that references it. Typed pools such as MODEL, PROVIDER_QUOTA, and INVOCATION_SLOT add metadata when a generic capacity shape is not enough; those details belong in later sections on this page.

whereRequirementsBelong.title: Where Requirements Belong
whereRequirementsBelong.body: Use this ownership split when you decide where to declare a pool and where to consume capacity. The normal authoring path is declare the pool at the top level, then reference that pool from the workstation you want to throttle.

howToUse.title: How To Use
howToUse.body: Declare shared pools under top-level resources, then reference those pool names from the workstation that should hold capacity while it runs. Use this page when you need the resources / bounded-concurrency reference before opening configuration, workers, or workstations sibling docs.

limitsAndAssumptions.title: Limits And Assumptions
limitsAndAssumptions.body: This page is the web resources / bounded-concurrency reference. It is not a verbatim sync of packaged CLI docs, not the configuration topology overview, not worker backends, not workstation routing or lifecycle, and not portability supportingFiles.

links.ownershipTopLevelShape: yes — {name, capacity} plus optional typed metadata
links.ownershipTopLevelUse: Declare the pool name and total available capacity
links.ownershipWorkstationUse: Consume capacity for a workflow step while that workstation runs
links.ownershipWorkerUse: Worker-runtime requirement metadata only; not the canonical workflow-step concurrency path
links.ownershipWorkerNote: Worker-only resources are not the canonical explanation for workflow-step concurrency. Put the scheduling-facing requirement on the workstation. Keep workers[].resources[] only when the worker-runtime contract itself needs the same requirement metadata.
links.ownershipSupportingFilesNote: Runtime-capacity resources are not portability supportingFiles. Do not overload a capacity pool with bundled files or external tool checks; supportingFiles belong to the portability contract, not to bounded concurrency.
